answer:
- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999)**  
  Across studies, people predicted that others were driven more by pay and security, and less by intrinsic rewards, than those others reported for themselves. This supports the idea that interesting work is often underweighted, but not that pay, role scope, or career capital should be ignored.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci (2000)**  
  This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs behind motivation and well-being. Interesting work matters because it often supports autonomy and competence, but the theory is broader than “do only the most interesting thing.”

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation robustly predicts performance, especially performance quality. It also finds that extrinsic incentives independently matter, so the evidence supports strong weighting of interesting work but not exclusive weighting.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
  This paper’s job characteristics model links skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback to internal motivation and satisfaction. It supports prioritizing challenging and meaningful work, while also emphasizing autonomy, feedback, and fit.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task significance, variety, feedback, complexity, and social support are strongly related to job satisfaction and motivation. It suggests “interesting technical content” is only one part of a broader good-job bundle.

- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that people are more satisfied when their jobs fit their vocational interests. The relationship is meaningful but not overwhelming, implying interest fit is important but should be traded off against other job attributes.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that vocational interests predict job and training performance, especially when interests align with the work environment. For research-heavy roles, interest likely supports persistence and excellence, but ability, opportunity, and incentives still matter.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile (1997)**  
  This paper argues that creativity is fostered by intrinsic motivation, autonomy, challenge, and supportive work environments. It is especially relevant to research work, where interesting problems can be a major driver of creative output.

- **“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant (2008)**  
  This paper shows that seeing the significance and beneficiaries of one’s work can increase motivation and performance. It suggests that leading a team could be motivating if it increases impact and responsibility, even if the technical content is less interesting.

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with pay satisfaction and a weak relationship with overall job satisfaction. It supports not overvaluing a salary bump, while still treating compensation as a real but limited factor.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
  This paper finds that emotional well-being generally rises with income for many people, though with diminishing and heterogeneous effects. It argues against dismissing salary entirely, but also against expecting a modest raise to dominate daily work experience.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009)**  
  This study finds that people who experience work as a calling gain identity and meaning from it. It also finds they may accept lower pay, worse conditions, and personal sacrifice, cautioning against optimizing almost exclusively for passion or meaning.

- **“Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)**  
  This paper shows that people overestimate the life-satisfaction impact of whatever factor is salient in a comparison. In a job decision, that warns against overfocusing on any single feature, whether interestingness, salary, title, or team leadership.

- **“What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?” — Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Szembrot (2012)**  
  This paper finds that predicted happiness strongly influences choices, but people also choose based on purpose, control, status, family welfare, and other values. It supports a pluralistic view of career decisions rather than an “interestingness almost exclusively” rule.

I think using bullet points might help to keep it organized, especially since they requested only relevant papers without any introduction.
answer:
- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007).**  
  This meta-analysis finds that motivational job features—autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity—strongly predict job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and performance. It supports prioritizing interesting, autonomous work, but also shows social support, feedback, and work context matter.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).**  
  This paper develops the Job Characteristics Model, arguing that meaningfulness, responsibility, and knowledge of results drive internal motivation and satisfaction. It suggests that intellectually rich, high-autonomy work can be unusually important, especially for people with high growth needs.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci (2005).**  
  This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs at work. It implies that interesting work and self-directed research are likely to support well-being and sustained motivation more than purely external rewards.

- **“The ‘What’ and ‘Why’ of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior” — Deci & Ryan (2000).**  
  This paper finds that intrinsic goals and autonomous motivation are more strongly linked to well-being than extrinsic goals such as money, status, or image. It supports your friends’ claim directionally, though it does not imply salary or role should be ignored.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014).**  
  This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially quality of work, while extrinsic incentives predict quantity more than quality. For research-heavy roles, it suggests interesting work may be especially valuable because quality and creativity matter.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999).**  
  This meta-analysis finds that expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. It suggests that a salary increase is valuable, but making pay or promotion the dominant reason for a move can sometimes crowd out motivation.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris (1987).**  
  This review finds broad evidence that enriched jobs are associated with higher satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It reinforces that interestingness, autonomy, and task significance are not soft perks but core predictors of work outcomes.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997).**  
  This paper distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, and finds that calling-oriented workers report greater satisfaction. If your current research feels like a calling, leaving for a less interesting but higher-status role may carry a real motivational cost.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005).**  
  This meta-analysis finds that fit with the job and organization predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. It suggests the best choice depends not only on “interesting work” but on fit with your values, desired identity, team, and growth path.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton (2010).**  
  This paper finds that higher income is associated with better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns beyond a high-income threshold in their data. It implies that a somewhat higher salary may matter less than work quality once compensation is already strong.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023).**  
  This paper revisits the income–well-being debate and finds that experienced well-being generally rises with income, though the pattern differs across happier and less happy people. It suggests salary is not irrelevant, but its marginal impact may be smaller than major differences in work meaning, autonomy, or fit.

- **“The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review” — Judge, Thoresen, Bono, & Patton (2001).**  
  This meta-analysis finds a meaningful positive relationship between job satisfaction and performance. If interesting research substantially increases your satisfaction, it may also help you do better work and build a stronger long-term career.

- **“Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” — Schkade & Kahneman (1998).**  
  This paper shows that people can overweight salient differences when predicting future happiness. In a career decision, visible features like salary, company brand, and title may be overweighted relative to daily work content—or, conversely, a currently salient desire to manage may be overweighted too.

- **“The Progress Principle: Using Small Wins to Ignite Joy, Engagement, and Creativity at Work” — Amabile & Kramer (2011).**  
  This work argues that making progress on meaningful work is one of the strongest drivers of positive inner work life. For research roles, the chance to make meaningful technical progress may be a particularly important source of motivation and creativity.

answer:
- **On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives** — Heath (1999)  
People systematically predict that others are driven more by pay, promotions, and external incentives than by intrinsic interest. This directly supports the idea that intrinsic features of work are often underrated, though it does not imply ignoring pay, role, or context.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)  
Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially for complex and quality-focused tasks, while extrinsic incentives matter more for quantity and routine output. For research-engineering-like work, the paper supports putting substantial weight on interestingness, but its “jointly” finding argues against focusing on it exclusively.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)  
Autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social features strongly predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. “Interesting work” matters partly because it bundles these features, but leadership, relationships, workload, and context can also change outcomes.

- **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci (2000)  
Well-being and sustained motivation depend heavily on autonomy, competence, and relatedness rather than external rewards alone. A job can be less intrinsically fascinating yet still good if it increases autonomy, mastery, and social connection through leadership.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially when they feel controlling, while informational feedback can enhance it. This cautions against treating higher pay as a clean substitute for intrinsically motivating work.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)  
Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, and people with substantially different salaries often report similar overall job satisfaction. This supports discounting a merely “bit higher” salary, especially at already-high compensation levels.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)  
Emotional well-being generally continues rising with income, but the slope is modest and there is evidence of flattening among the least-happy subgroup. This argues that money still matters, but not enough to make daily work quality irrelevant.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. The relevant question is not only “which work is more interesting,” but where your interests, desired identity, management aspirations, and environment fit best.

- **Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)  
People perform better when their vocational interests match their work environment, with especially relevant implications for complex, self-directed work. This supports the idea that doing work you find genuinely interesting can compound into better long-run performance.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis** — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay (2019)  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. The paper supports weighting meaning and interest heavily, while also noting that meaningful work can coexist with stress and should not be romanticized.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)  
People who experience work as a calling tend to report higher satisfaction with both work and life. The implication is that unusually interesting or mission-linked work can be a major asset, but career orientation differs across people and roles.

- **Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work** — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001)  
Employees can change task, relational, and cognitive boundaries to make their jobs more meaningful and engaging. This is relevant because a less interesting leadership role might become more interesting if it gives you room to shape the work and team.

- **The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions** — Grant (2008)  
Experiencing the impact of one’s work on others can increase motivation and performance. A small-team lead role may add meaning through mentoring and influence, even if the underlying technical area is less exciting.

- **Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do** — Amabile (1997)  
Intrinsic motivation is central to creativity, and organizational environments affect whether people can sustain it. For research-oriented work, the paper supports protecting genuine interest and autonomy as inputs to creative output.

- **Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)  
People overpredict the importance of salient but limited life differences because they focus on them when making comparisons. In job choice, salary, title, and company brand may be overweighted relative to the day-to-day experience of the work.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.**  
  Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports putting major weight on whether the work itself is engaging, meaningful, and growth-producing.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.**  
  A large meta-analysis finds that motivating work characteristics strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It also finds that social context and work conditions matter, so “interesting work” is important but not the only variable.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This supports caution about optimizing too much for salary or external status when the core work is intrinsically motivating.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci, 2000.**  
  Well-being and high-quality motivation depend heavily on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research work matters most when it also gives ownership, mastery, and strong collaborators.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.**  
  Vocational interests predict job performance and training performance, especially when interests fit the job. This is strong evidence that doing work you find genuinely interesting can have career-performance value, not just happiness value.

- **“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover” — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka & Lanivich, 2011.**  
  Interest fit is associated with better performance and lower turnover. The effect is real but not overwhelming, implying interest should be weighted heavily rather than exclusively.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and intent to quit. Interesting work is part of fit, but culture, manager quality, and team fit are separate predictors.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those who mainly view work as a job or career ladder. This supports prioritizing meaningful, intrinsically absorbing work when that orientation fits you.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.**  
  Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. This suggests that, especially above a comfortable baseline, a somewhat higher salary may matter less than the daily quality of the work.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010.**  
  Higher income predicts better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in their U.S. data. This supports not overvaluing marginal salary increases if both options are already highly paid.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.**  
  Emotional well-being generally continues rising with income, but with diminishing returns and important individual differences. This weakens the claim that money stops mattering, but still leaves room for work content to dominate at high income levels.

- **“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages” — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger, 2018.**  
  Workers place large monetary value on nonwage job attributes such as autonomy, schedule control, meaningfulness, and working conditions. This supports treating work quality as economically valuable, not merely as a nice-to-have.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**

answer:
**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
Jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback tend to produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing the work features that make a role interesting, but not treating “interesting” as the only dimension.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
Autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity, social support, and context all predict satisfaction, motivation, performance, and withdrawal. Stimulating work matters a lot, but team, manager, and organizational context also matter.

**Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis**  
People whose vocational interests fit their jobs report higher job satisfaction across many studies. This is strong evidence that “will I find the work interesting?” is a major career variable.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research**  
Vocational interests predict job and training performance, especially when the work environment matches the person’s interests. Interesting work can therefore compound into better skill growth, persistence, and achievement.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
Intrinsic motivation is especially predictive of performance quality, while extrinsic incentives are more predictive of performance quantity. The evidence argues against both “optimize for salary/status only” and “ignore external incentives entirely.”

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This supports being cautious about letting pay or status dominate a choice involving creative or research work.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest compared with many people’s intuitions. A small salary increase is unlikely to compensate for a large decline in work content or fit.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved**  
Higher income is associated with higher experienced well-being for most people, though the relationship is weaker or flatter for some less-happy groups. Money is not irrelevant, but marginal income gains usually should not dominate daily work experience.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
Fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, turnover intentions, and performance. Interesting tasks are only one part of fit; team quality, culture, manager, and leadership opportunity also count.

**The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review**  
Job satisfaction is meaningfully related to job performance, especially when satisfaction and performance are measured well. Enjoying and valuing the work can be instrumentally useful, not just emotionally pleasant.

**A Meta-Analysis of Burnout with Job Demands, Resources, and Attitudes**  
Burnout is associated with high demands, low resources, low control, low support, and negative work attitudes. Fascinating work can still be a poor long-run choice if it is unsustainable.

**Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead**  
People differ in their intrinsic and identity-based motivation to lead, and these differences relate to leadership potential. If leading a small team is something you genuinely want, that is itself an intrinsic-fit signal rather than merely an external perk.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
People who experience work as a calling tend to report higher job and life satisfaction. However, meaning can also make people tolerate sacrifices, so “interesting and meaningful” should be balanced against conditions and boundaries.

**On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review**  
Meaningful work comes from self-concordance, values, purpose, belonging, and contribution. “Interesting” is important, but meaningfulness can also come from leadership, mission, community, autonomy, and impact.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)  
Shows that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the importance of interesting/enriched work, but frames “interesting” as a bundle of work-design features rather than novelty alone.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)  
Meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support are strongly related to satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and performance. It strongly supports caring about the day-to-day content of work, while also showing that social context and workload matter.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci (2000)  
Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research work often satisfies autonomy and competence, but a leadership role could also satisfy them if the role is designed well.

**Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover** — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka & Lanivich (2011)  
Finds that fit between a person’s interests and their work predicts performance and turnover beyond ability and personality. This is direct evidence that “interest” is not just a soft preference but a meaningful career variable.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)  
Person–job and person–organization fit are strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to quit. The paper supports evaluating daily work, values, team, and environment rather than optimizing only salary or title.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)  
Pay level and pay satisfaction are positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction. This suggests that a higher salary matters, but often less than people expect once basic expectations and fairness are met.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006)  
Shows that people often overestimate the effect of income on happiness because income becomes highly salient when they think about it. The implication is that offer comparisons can overweight salary and underweight daily lived experience.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)  
Finds that emotional well-being generally rises with income, but with diminishing returns and important heterogeneity across people. This weakens simplistic “money does not matter” claims while still not supporting salary as the dominant criterion.

**People Who Choose Time Over Money Are Happier** — Hershfield, Mogilner & Barnea (2016)  
Across multiple studies, people who prioritize time over money report higher happiness, controlling for income and demographics. This supports treating time, attention, and preferred daily activity as major inputs to well-being.

**Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want** — Wilson & Gilbert (2005)  
Reviews evidence that people systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of future emotions. In job decisions, salient features like compensation, prestige, or being a manager may be overforecast, while daily intellectual stimulation may be underforecast.

**The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art** — Bakker & Demerouti (2007)  
Shows that engagement and burnout depend on the balance between job demands and job resources such as autonomy, support, feedback, and growth. A leadership role may add autonomy and impact, but also demands, meetings, and stress.

**Meaningful Work: A Review and Research Agenda** — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski (2010)  
Reviews evidence that meaningful work comes from self-realization, purpose, belonging, and perceived impact. It supports prioritizing meaningful and interesting work, but not “almost exclusively,” because meaning has multiple sources.

answer:
- **“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover”** — Van Iddekinge et al., 2011  
  Vocational interests and interest–job fit predict employee performance and lower turnover, with real but not overwhelming effect sizes. This directly supports taking intellectually interesting work seriously, but not treating it as the only variable.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do”** — Amabile, 1997  
  Intrinsic motivation is a major driver of creativity, and controlling extrinsic pressures can suppress creative output. For research and innovation roles, losing interesting/autonomous work can be a large cost even if pay or title improves.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation”** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999  
  Expected tangible rewards often reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when rewards feel controlling. The result cautions against equating higher pay/status with better motivation, while also noting that supportive feedback or non-controlling rewards need not be harmful.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”** — Ryan & Deci, 2000  
  The theory argues that wellbeing and high-quality motivation come from autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research may satisfy autonomy and competence, while leading a team may satisfy relatedness and impact, so the theory favors need-fit rather than “interest only.”

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory”** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976  
  Skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. A leadership role could improve some of these dimensions, but less interesting technical content could reduce others.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007  
  This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, social support, and work context all matter for satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It implies that “interesting work” is important but should be compared against autonomy, team quality, leadership scope, and day-to-day demands.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005  
  Fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. This suggests comparing both roles on overall fit, not just salary, prestige, or task interestingness.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work”** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997  
  People who experience work as a calling report more meaning and satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career ladder. It supports valuing meaningful/interesting work, but the evidence is correlational and does not prove one should optimize for it almost exclusively.

- **“Does Intrinsic Motivation Fuel the Prosocial Fire? Motivational Synergy in Predicting Persistence, Performance, and Productivity”** — Grant, 2008  
  Across field studies, intrinsic motivation predicted persistence, performance, and productivity, especially when combined with prosocial motivation. Leadership may add impact and relatedness, but the strongest pattern is interest plus impact rather than impact replacing interest.

- **“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion”** — Vallerand et al., 2003  
  Harmonious passion is associated with wellbeing and adaptive engagement, while obsessive passion is associated with conflict and rigidity. This supports following genuine interest, but warns against making any single passion dominate life decisions too completely.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion”** — Kahneman et al., 2006  
  The paper argues that people often overestimate the wellbeing gains from higher income because money becomes overly salient during comparison. It supports discounting a modest salary increase relative to daily work experience, though it is about income generally rather than research-career choices specifically.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved”** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023  
  The paper finds that experienced wellbeing generally continues rising with income for many people, while plateaus are more concentrated among less-happy subgroups. It cautions against ignoring salary entirely, but still leaves room for job quality and motivation to dominate when salary differences are modest.

- **“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages”** — Maestas et al., 2023  
  Workers are willing to give up meaningful amounts of pay for non-wage job attributes such as schedule control, telework, safety, and better working conditions. This implies that non-salary features can rationally outweigh higher pay, but those features include more than just intellectual interest.

- **“Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis”** — Ng, Eby, Sorensen & Feldman, 2005  
  Objective career success, such as salary and promotion, and subjective career success, such as satisfaction, have overlapping but distinct predictors. A move that improves title, pay, and management scope may improve objective success while still reducing subjective success if it worsens fit or intrinsic motivation.

answer:
- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for complex, high-quality work. This supports weighting interesting research work heavily, but not “almost exclusively.”

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).** Controlling tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. The implication is that salary/status-driven moves can be costly if they make work feel less autonomous, though fair or informational rewards need not be harmful.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile (1997).** Intrinsic task motivation is central to creativity, especially in open-ended problem solving. For research engineering, a less interesting agenda may reduce creative output unless offset by autonomy, resources, or new forms of challenge.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).** Autonomy, task significance, feedback, task variety, and social support are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. “Interesting work” matters, but it is one component of broader job design.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. A team-lead role could be more motivating than it first appears if it increases ownership, impact, and autonomy.

- **“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover” — Van Iddekinge et al. (2011).** Vocational interest fit predicts job performance, training performance, and lower turnover, even beyond some other individual-difference measures. The effects are real but not overwhelming, so interest should be a major input rather than the only input.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).** Person–job fit is strongly tied to satisfaction, while organization, supervisor, and group fit also matter for commitment and retention. The decision should consider research fit, leadership fit, company fit, manager fit, and team fit separately.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al. (2010).** Pay level is positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction, while pay satisfaction matters more. At already-high compensation levels, a small salary increase may be less important than day-to-day work quality, but it is not irrelevant.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman et al. (2006).** People tend to overestimate how much income differences will affect experienced happiness because income is salient when comparing lives. This supports skepticism toward choosing mainly for a bit more pay, especially once financial needs are well covered.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).** Well-being generally rises with income, but with diminishing returns and important heterogeneity across people. More money can still matter, but it is unlikely to uniformly compensate for a large loss in daily interest or meaning.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009).** People who experience work as a calling often accept lower pay, harder conditions, and personal sacrifice. The paper warns against romanticizing interesting or meaningful work so much that you ignore exploitation, burnout, or career capital.

- **“Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead” — Chan & Drasgow (2001).** Motivation to lead is a real individual difference and predicts leadership emergence and development. If leading a team is something you deeply want, that may be an intrinsic-interest factor rather than merely an extrinsic promotion factor.

- **“Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model” — Sheldon & Elliot (1999).** Goals aligned with one’s authentic interests and values produce more sustained effort, progress, and well-being. The relevant question is not just which job is “more interesting,” but which choice better matches your self-directed long-term aims.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Google - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
**Researching interesting work**

The user is asking whether focusing on interesting things is crucial, so I'll need to look up relevant papers. I have to provide paper names along with two-sentence summaries, focusing on concepts like job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and autonomy without adding any extra prose. 

This means I should gather studies that highlight intrinsic motivation's importance for satisfaction and performance, while also noting how extrinsic factors like salary have diminishing returns after a certain point. I’ll strive for accuracy in my findings.
**Evaluating intrinsic focus**

I think saying to focus "almost exclusively" on interesting work is an overstatement. Values, autonomy, competence, and career capital also matter significantly.

In reviewing relevant papers, one good source is "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation" (Ryan & Deci, 2000), which indicates that while intrinsic motivation is critical, competence and relatedness also play an important role.

I should also include "A Meta-Analytic Review" (Deci et al., 1999/2001), which reveals that extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, and "Interest as a Predictor..." (Nye et al., 2012) discusses how vocational interests influence performance and success.
**Evaluating income and well-being**

I’m considering how income can boost life evaluation, noting that daily emotional well-being seems to plateau around $75,000 in the US, though there's some debate about this. It looks like money matters but with diminishing returns. I should also mention studies showing that experienced well-being can rise with income, even above that threshold. There’s a discussion about happiness being linked to productivity too, which is interesting but might be less relevant. Then there's the meaning of work, emphasizing that meaningful work enhances motivation and well-being beyond mere financial aspects.
answer:
**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being — Ryan & Deci, 2000.**  
Intrinsic motivation, autonomy, competence, and relatedness are robust predictors of well-being and sustained performance. This supports prioritizing interesting, self-directed work, but not “almost exclusively,” because social context and competence growth also matter.

**Interest as a Predictor of Academic and Career Choice, Performance, and Success: A Meta-Analytic Review — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.**  
Vocational interests predict career choice, persistence, performance, and success across many studies. The effects are meaningful but not total, so interest is important rather than the only rational criterion.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
Person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. If OpenAI gives substantially better fit with your interests and abilities, the evidence says that is a major point in its favor.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.**  
Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback tend to create higher internal motivation and satisfaction. “Interesting work” matters partly because it often bundles these motivating job characteristics.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.**  
Pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the association is relatively modest. A somewhat higher salary is unlikely to fully offset a large drop in intrinsic interest or job fit.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010.**  
Income is more strongly related to life evaluation than to day-to-day emotional well-being, with diminishing returns for daily affect in their data. This suggests salary matters, but less than people often expect once financial needs are comfortably met.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year — Killingsworth, 2021.**  
Using experience-sampling data, this paper finds that well-being continues to rise with income beyond previously claimed plateaus. This weakens any simplistic claim that money stops mattering, though it still does not show that salary dominates work content.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006.**  
People tend to overestimate the happiness impact of salient factors like income because they focus attention on them when deciding. Applied to career choice, salary and title may loom larger beforehand than the daily experience of the work itself.

**The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger, 2018.**  
Workers place substantial monetary value on nonwage job attributes such as autonomy, meaningful work, schedule flexibility, and pleasant conditions. This supports treating job quality as economically significant, not as a soft afterthought.

**The Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski, 2010.**  
Meaningful work is linked to motivation, engagement, identity, and well-being. Interesting research may matter because it provides meaning, mastery, and identity, but leadership can also provide meaning if it fits your values.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
Certain tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting, especially when experienced as controlling. This cautions against letting compensation incentives crowd out intrinsically motivating work.

**The Value of Bosses — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**  
Good managers substantially improve worker productivity, indicating that leading a team can be high-impact rather than merely administrative. If you intrinsically want to lead and develop people, the Google role may offer a different kind of meaningful work rather than simply “less interesting” work.

**Considering income and well-being**

I'm thinking that while high salaries might provide status and security, the incremental benefits could be limited. Research suggests that well-being increases with income, even beyond $75,000 per year, highlighting that money does matter, but it isn’t the sole factor in satisfaction. I wonder if including both financial and non-financial elements in life could create a better balance. This makes me think about "Affective Forecasting" from Wilson & Gilbert, which could add to this discussion.
**Reviewing research papers**

I’m considering the implications of research on happiness and work success. One paper is "The Benefits of Frequent Positive Affect: Does Happiness Lead to Success?" by Lyubomirsky et al. from 2005. It suggests that happy workers might perform better, though the relationship isn't direct. There's also "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" by Wrzesniewski et al., which finds that those framing work as a calling experience higher satisfaction. This could support the notion of pursuing meaningful work.
**Clarifying summary requirements**

I want to create a summary that’s exactly two sentences, avoiding any introductions or conclusions. I’m considering including around 10 to 12 papers, aiming to be concise but informative, ensuring I list the papers along with their summaries. I should conclude that the overall findings support a strong but not exclusive perspective. However, I have to ensure that my conclusions are supported by the evidence presented in the summaries.
**Considering job satisfaction factors**

I think it’s important to include how employees perceive their work as meaningful. There's a piece titled "The Value of Working Conditions..." that touches on economic aspects. Also, another study discusses "The Relative Importance of Job Satisfaction Factors and Their Impacts on Overall Job Satisfaction," which might provide nuanced insights. I'll want to summarize these points in a way that reflects the deeper complexities of job satisfaction and its impacts on employees.
answer:
**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci, 2000. This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs underlying motivation and well-being. It supports heavily weighting interesting, autonomy-supportive work, but also implies team leadership could be valuable if it satisfies competence and relatedness.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007. This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It supports prioritizing day-to-day work content over headline variables like salary or employer prestige.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014. Intrinsic motivation robustly predicts performance, especially performance quality, while extrinsic incentives more often predict quantity. This supports the importance of interesting work, but not the claim that extrinsic factors should be ignored.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999. Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks people already find interesting. This is relevant if a higher-paying but less interesting role shifts motivation away from curiosity and mastery, though it does not mean pay is always harmful.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010. Pay level has only a modest relationship with overall job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. This suggests a somewhat higher salary should usually not dominate a decision when both options already meet financial needs.

**Work Values and Job Rewards: A Theory of Job Satisfaction** — Kalleberg, 1977. Job satisfaction depends on the match between what a person values and what the job provides, with intrinsic rewards often being especially important. It argues against a universal rule: if leading people is a core personal value, that may matter alongside technical interestingness.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — Schkade & Kahneman, 1998. People overestimate the happiness impact of salient differences because they focus on them when making judgments. In job choice, this warns against overweighting visible features like salary, title, or company brand relative to daily lived experience.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006. The paper argues that people overestimate the effect of income on experienced happiness and underweight how they actually spend their time. This supports prioritizing the everyday texture of the job, while not implying money has zero value.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** — Killingsworth, 2021. This study finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income across a broad income range. It qualifies the “focus almost exclusively on interesting work” view by showing compensation can still matter measurably.

**The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages** — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger, 2023. Workers place substantial monetary value on non-wage job attributes such as autonomy, schedule control, and other working conditions. This supports comparing the roles as bundles of daily work, autonomy, leadership, and meaning rather than mainly as salary differences.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997. People differ in whether they see work primarily as a job, career, or calling, and calling-oriented workers report greater fulfillment. This supports choosing intrinsically meaningful work, while also recognizing that advancement and leadership can be central for career-oriented people.

**Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being: A Meta-Analysis** — Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010. Job satisfaction is substantially associated with overall subjective well-being. Because work satisfaction spills into life satisfaction, interesting and satisfying daily work is not a minor consideration.

answer:
**On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives**  
Shows that people and managers tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by pay and other extrinsic incentives relative to intrinsic motives. This supports the idea that interesting work is often underrated, but not that other job attributes should be ignored.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do**  
Reviews evidence that creativity is strongest when people are intrinsically motivated by interest, enjoyment, and challenge in the work itself. For research-heavy work, this argues for heavily weighting intellectual interest and autonomy.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
Finds that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. “Interesting work” matters partly because it creates these conditions, but a leadership role could also provide some of them.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
Reviews evidence that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs tied to motivation and well-being. It supports prioritizing intrinsically motivating work, but frames the target as need satisfaction rather than interestingness alone.

**The “What” and “Why” of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior**  
Finds that intrinsic aspirations and autonomous motives predict better well-being than extrinsic aspirations such as wealth or image. This favors roles aligned with curiosity and values, while leaving room for leadership if it is genuinely self-endorsed.

**Self-Concordance, Goal Attainment, and the Pursuit of Happiness: Can There Be an Upward Spiral?**  
Shows that people are more likely to attain goals, and gain more well-being from them, when the goals fit their interests and values. The key question is not only which job is more interesting, but which path feels more self-concordant after novelty fades.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
Finds that people differ in whether they view work mainly as a job, career, or calling, with calling-oriented workers deriving deeper fulfillment from the work itself. This supports taking meaningful and interesting work seriously, but implies the optimal choice depends on your own work orientation.

**Person–Environment Fit: A Meta-Analysis of Its Relationships with Job Attitudes and Performance**  
Shows that person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. Interesting work is one major dimension of fit, but culture, collaborators, manager quality, and role expectations also matter.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
Finds that pay level has a positive but relatively modest relationship with job satisfaction. This supports not overweighting a small-to-moderate salary increase, especially if both options already meet financial needs.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
Finds that higher income is strongly related to life evaluation, but much less related to day-to-day emotional well-being above a moderate income level. For already-high earners, marginal salary gains may matter less than daily work quality.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
Finds, using experience-sampling data, that experienced well-being continues to rise with income above the earlier proposed plateau. This is a useful counterweight to “ignore money,” suggesting pay still matters even if it should not dominate.

**Happiness: Adaptation to Income and to Status in an Individual Panel**  
Finds evidence that people adapt substantially to income changes, while relative status effects may be more persistent. This makes a salary bump less decisive if it mainly changes consumption, but suggests a role/status change like leading a team may have durable value.

**The Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Life Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis**  
Shows a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction. Because work strongly spills over into life, day-to-day role quality is unlikely to be a minor consideration.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction**  
Shows that people overweight salient differences when imagining alternatives, leading to mistaken predictions about happiness. In your case, research excitement, salary, and team leadership could each be over-weighted if they are the feature currently in focus.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** This classic paper finds that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation, satisfaction, and performance. It supports prioritizing intrinsically engaging work, but not “interestingness” alone.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris (1987).** This meta-analysis finds reliable links between enriched job characteristics and job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It suggests interesting, autonomous, feedback-rich work matters a lot, especially for people with strong growth needs.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci (2005).** This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive high-quality motivation and well-being at work. Interesting work helps when it supports these needs, but leadership, team context, and autonomy can also satisfy them.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005).** Person–job fit and person–organization fit strongly predict job satisfaction, commitment, turnover intentions, and performance. This supports taking day-to-day research fit seriously, while also weighing team, manager, and organizational fit.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012).** Vocational interests predict job and training performance, especially when people’s interests match their work environment. This is strong evidence that genuinely interesting work is not a luxury but a performance-relevant factor.

- **“Optimal Experience in Work and Leisure” — Csikszentmihalyi & LeFevre (1989).** Experience-sampling data show that flow often occurs at work when challenge and skill are well matched. Deeply interesting technical work may create flow, but a leadership role can too if it provides the right challenge-skill balance.

- **“Job, Career, and Calling: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997).** People differ in whether they experience work mainly as a job, career, or calling. If research feels like a calling, losing interestingness can be costly; if leading people feels like a calling, management may have intrinsic value.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010).** Pay level has only a modest relationship with overall job satisfaction. This suggests that a somewhat higher salary should usually not dominate differences in work content, autonomy, or fit.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton (2010).** Higher income predicts better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being shows much weaker gains after comfortable income levels. This implies extra salary may matter less than the daily experience of the work.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023).** This paper reconciles conflicting income findings by showing that well-being usually keeps rising with income, but not equally for everyone. Pay matters, but it is not a reliable substitute for work that is engaging and psychologically healthy.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw, & Stanton (2015).** Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity, showing that managers can create substantial value. A team-lead role may therefore be meaningful and impactful even if the object-level work is less intellectually exciting.

- **“Business-Unit-Level Relationship Between Employee Satisfaction, Employee Engagement, and Business Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis” — Harter, Schmidt, & Hayes (2002).** Engagement and satisfaction are associated with productivity, profitability, customer satisfaction, and retention. Interesting work can drive engagement, but engagement also depends on management, resources, recognition, and team environment.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art” — Bakker & Demerouti (2007).** Engagement is predicted by resources like autonomy, feedback, support, and development opportunities, while burnout is predicted by excessive demands. This argues against optimizing almost exclusively for interestingness without considering sustainability and support.

- **“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want” — Wilson & Gilbert (2005).** People often mispredict the intensity and duration of future feelings from major choices. For a job decision, this suggests comparing concrete daily routines, coworkers, responsibilities, and growth paths rather than relying only on salient labels like “more interesting” or “team lead.”

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich find that pay level has only a weak-to-moderate relationship with job satisfaction. This suggests a modest salary increase should usually be weighed against role quality, intrinsic motivation, and fit.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”** — Ryan and Deci argue that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central to motivation and well-being. Interesting research may satisfy these needs, but leading a team could also satisfy them if it offers real autonomy, mastery, and connection.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation”** — Deci, Koestner, and Ryan find that controlling extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This supports being cautious about trading highly engaging work for pay or status alone.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis”** — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, and Tay find that meaningful work is associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal. Meaningful and interesting work are not identical, but both appear to be major predictors of a good work life.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work”** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, and Schwartz show that people who experience work as a calling report higher fulfillment than those who see it mainly as a job or career. The finding favors work that feels intrinsically compelling, but also shows that meaning depends on personal framing and context.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson find that fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This argues against focusing only on task interestingness and for evaluating broader fit.

- **“Job Demands, Job Resources, and Their Relationship with Burnout and Engagement: A Multi-Sample Study”** — Schaufeli and Bakker show that job resources such as autonomy, feedback, support, and learning opportunities promote engagement, while excessive demands increase burnout. A leadership role could be better or worse depending on whether it increases resources more than demands.

- **“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion”** — Vallerand and colleagues distinguish harmonious passion, which predicts well-being and persistence, from obsessive passion, which predicts conflict and rigidity. This supports following interesting work, but not turning interest into a single overriding rule.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?”** — O’Keefe, Dweck, and Walton find that believing interests are fixed can narrow exploration and reduce persistence when difficulty arises. This suggests that a less-obviously-interesting leadership path could become interesting through growth, mastery, and responsibility.

- **“Understanding Contemporary Forms of Exploitation: Attributions of Passion Serve to Legitimize the Poor Treatment of Workers”** — Kim, Campbell, Shepherd, and Kay find that people see poor treatment of passionate workers as more acceptable because passion is assumed to compensate for bad conditions. This is a warning against using “interesting work” to justify ignoring pay, workload, or career leverage.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman et al. (2006).** People tend to overestimate how much income differences will affect their day-to-day happiness because money becomes overly salient during comparison. The paper supports being cautious about overweighting a salary increase relative to daily work experience.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al. (2010).** Pay level has only a weak-to-moderate relationship with job satisfaction, especially compared with richer features of the work itself. This suggests higher salary matters, but usually less than people expect.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin, and Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives can predict performance quantity. The key finding is not “only interesting work matters,” but that intrinsic interest is a major independent driver.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner, and Ryan (1999).** Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks people already find interesting. This supports caution about trading intrinsically motivating work for more externally rewarded work.

- **“Self-Determination Theory in Work Organizations: The State of a Science” — Deci, Olafsen, and Ryan (2017).** Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central predictors of motivation, well-being, and work quality. A role that reduces intellectual autonomy may be costly even if it improves pay or status.

- **“The Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried and Ferris (1987).** Autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback are consistently associated with job satisfaction and internal motivation. “Interesting work” matters most when it also provides autonomy, challenge, and visible impact.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson (2005).** Person–job and person–organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. The evidence favors optimizing for fit across multiple dimensions, not just salary, title, or abstract interestingness.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan et al. (2019).** Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and lower withdrawal intentions. The evidence supports taking meaning seriously, though much of it is correlational rather than causal.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski et al. (1997).** People differ in whether they experience work mainly as a job, career, or calling. Those with a calling orientation report higher work and life satisfaction, suggesting that intrinsically meaningful work can be unusually important for some people.

- **“Motivation and Creativity: Effects of Motivational Orientation on Creative Writers” — Amabile (1985).** Intrinsic motivation improved creative output, while extrinsic framing tended to reduce creativity. This is especially relevant for research roles where creativity and originality are central.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout” — Demerouti et al. (2001).** Burnout is driven by high demands combined with insufficient resources such as autonomy, support, and growth opportunities. A leadership role may increase meaning and control, but also demands and stress.

- **“People Who Choose Time Over Money Are Happier” — Hershfield, Mogilner, and Barnea (2016).** People who prioritize time over money tend to report greater happiness. This suggests that day-to-day experience and control over time are often more important than marginal compensation.

- **“Affective Forecasting” — Wilson and Gilbert (2003).** People systematically mispredict how future events will affect their happiness, often overweighting salient changes like money, status, or title. This supports skepticism about intuitive predictions in career decisions.

answer:
- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999).**  
  People systematically overestimate how much others are motivated by money and underestimate intrinsic motives like enjoyment, meaning, and challenge. This supports the claim that interesting work can be underrated, though it does not imply it should be the only factor.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).**  
  Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially for complex, high-quality, cognitively demanding work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence favors weighting interesting work heavily but not exclusively.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile (1997).**  
  Intrinsic motivation is central to creativity, especially when work is complex, open-ended, and research-like. The paper also emphasizes autonomy, resources, support, and challenge, not just raw interest.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).**  
  Autonomy, task significance, feedback, variety, and complexity are strongly related to job satisfaction and motivation. “Interesting work” matters, but it is part of a broader job-design bundle.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris (1987).**  
  Skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. This supports prioritizing intellectually rich work, especially when paired with autonomy.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci (2005).**  
  Work is most motivating when it supports autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A leadership role could satisfy some of these needs, while less interesting work could reduce them.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).**  
  Fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This suggests the right answer depends on overall fit, not only salary or interestingness.

- **“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover” — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka & Lanivich (2011).**  
  Vocational interests are meaningfully related to job performance and training outcomes. This supports taking “interestingness” seriously for long-term excellence.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).**  
  Interests predict performance and persistence across educational and occupational settings. The evidence favors choosing work aligned with deep interests, but not ignoring other constraints.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997).**  
  People who experience work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction. The paper also shows that meaning is subjective, so leading a team could itself become meaningful even if the technical work is less interesting.

- **“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003).**  
  Harmonious passion is associated with flow, persistence, and well-being. Obsessive passion can create conflict and costs, so “follow what is interesting” is healthiest when it remains balanced.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).**  
  Pay has a positive but relatively modest association with job satisfaction. A somewhat higher salary alone is unlikely to dominate intrinsic motivation, but it is not irrelevant.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006).**  
  People often overestimate how much higher income will improve day-to-day happiness. This cautions against overweighting salary when comparing jobs.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015).**  
  Bosses have large measurable effects on worker productivity, and good managers create substantial value. This makes the team-lead opportunity meaningful, though the paper does not show that managing is personally preferable to research.

- **“Focalism: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting” — Wilson, Wheatley, Meyers, Gilbert & Axsom (2000).**  
  People overpredict the long-term emotional impact of whichever feature is most salient during a decision. This cautions against letting any single dimension—interesting work, salary, or leadership—dominate the choice.

The evidence supports prioritizing meaningful and interesting work highly, but not “almost exclusively” over fit, autonomy, relationships, impact, and financial needs.
